case_metadata.court: Appellate Court of Illinois, First District, First Division

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: In 2008, Vanderhoof presented on several occasions to his primary care physician, Dr. David Lerner, complaining of heartburn, stomach pain, back pain, nausea, and diarrhea.
evidence_index.summary: Vanderhoof presented with symptoms of gallbladder disease in 2008.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: On January 12, 2009, after receiving preoperative clearance from Dr. Lerner, Vanderhoof underwent the cholecystectomy at Glenbrook Hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Berk initially proceeded with a laparoscopic procedure, but soon converted to an open procedure after encountering significant inflammation around the gallbladder.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Berk converted to open procedure due to inflammation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Berk transected, or cut through, what he thought was the cystic duct; instead, it turned out, the severed structure was the common bile duct.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Berk mistakenly cut the common bile duct.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Following his surgery, Vanderhoof suffered from an intermittent bile leak, which required the insertion of two drains.
evidence_index.summary: Post-surgery, Vanderhoof had a bile leak requiring drains.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Toward the end of this hospitalization, he became severely septic, and passed away on March 19.
evidence_index.summary: Vanderhoof became septic and died on March 19.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Finks testified that the top priority in performing a gallbladder dissection is to make sure the common bile duct is not cut.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Finks emphasized avoiding cutting the common bile duct.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jonathan Finks

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Finks opined to a reasonable degree of medical certainty, however, that Dr. Berk violated the standard of care by transecting the duct without first taking any of these four precautionary steps.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Finks stated Dr. Berk violated standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jonathan Finks

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Finks concluded, to a reasonable degree of medical certainty, that Dr. Berk’s transection of Vanderhoof’s common bile duct, and nothing else, caused the bile leak and sepsis that eventually led to his death.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Finks linked bile duct transection to death.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jonathan Finks

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Jennifer Foster was Dr. Berk’s physician’s assistant during Vanderhoof’s surgery. She testified that it was “incredibly difficult” to see the anatomy from the beginning of the surgery.
evidence_index.summary: Foster noted difficulty in seeing anatomy during surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Jennifer Foster

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dr. Berk testified that Vanderhoof’s cholecystectomy was “[p]robably” one of the more difficult he had ever performed on a patient.
evidence_index.summary: Dr. Berk described the surgery as very difficult.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Richard Berk

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Following deliberations, on February 1, 2013, the jury returned a verdict in favor of plaintiff and against defendants Berk and NorthShore.
evidence_index.summary: Jury verdict in favor of plaintiff against defendants.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: We affirm the judgment of the circuit court.
evidence_index.summary: Judgment of the circuit court affirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

verdict_detected.key_phrase: the jury returned a verdict in favor of plaintiff and against defendants

gaps_identified: Detailed timeline of post-surgery events unclear

initial_state.chief_complaint: Heartburn, stomach pain, back pain, nausea, and diarrhea
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented on several occasions with symptoms suggestive of gallbladder disease.

reveal.result_summary: Conversion to open procedure due to inflammation

decision_points.prompt: What precautionary steps should be taken to avoid cutting the common bile duct?

options.description: Identify anatomical landmarks and use intraoperative cholangiography
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to avoid bile duct injury

options.description: Proceed with dissection without additional precautions
options.clinical_reasoning: Increases risk of bile duct injury

expected_action_court.description: Precautionary steps should have been taken
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated standard of care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to take precautionary steps led to bile duct injury
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Precautionary steps should have been taken to avoid cutting the common bile duct

decision_points.prompt: How should the postoperative bile leak be managed?

options.description: Insert drains and monitor for sepsis

actual_action_defendant.description: Drains were inserted to manage bile leak

expected_action_court.description: Drains should be inserted to manage bile leak

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Not applicable as the correct action was taken
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Drains should be inserted to manage bile leak

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with symptoms suggestive of gallbladder disease

key_events.event: Bile leak observed and drains inserted

key_findings: Breach of duty in transecting the common bile duct
Causation established - bile duct transection led to sepsis and death

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to avoid cutting the common bile duct
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Precautionary steps would have prevented bile duct injury

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents with symptoms suggestive of gallbladder disease
Cholecystectomy performed, converted to open procedure

allegations.allegation: Failure to avoid cutting the common bile duct
